topic: Two people discover they've been working on identical projects
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the shared lab hummed, a grating soundtrack to my growing unease. I stared at the blueprint spread across the steel table, the intricate schematics for the 'Aether Drive' staring back at me, identical to the one I’d spent the last three weeks meticulously drafting. My jaw tightened, a muscle jumping beneath my skin. I picked up my pen, the plastic feeling slick and alien in my hand, and savagely crossed out a line of code, the graphite scratching loudly against the paper. "Impossible," I muttered, the sound sharp and brittle in the quiet room. I slammed the pen down, the sudden noise echoing off the metal shelving. I spun around, scanning the empty workspace, my eyes darting to the corner where I knew Dr. Aris was supposed to be. He wasn't there. The realization settled in my stomach like a cold stone, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I walked into the conference room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the polished floor. The presentation screen displayed the finalized design mock-up—the very one I’d spent sleepless nights perfecting. Then I saw it: a preliminary sketch of the same component layout, watermarked with a faint, almost invisible signature that was undeniably mine. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too large for my lungs. I gripped the edge of the mahogany table until my knuckles turned white. "What in the world?" I demanded, my voice pitched higher than intended, laced with a tremor of disbelief. I didn't look at the projected image; I looked at the empty chair across from me, a silent accusation hanging in the air between us. Every instinct screamed that this was a deliberate, calculated deception, and the heat rising in my cheeks was entirely involuntary.

[story 3]

It was the sheer audacity of it that made my hands shake. I found the data logs, the raw telemetry readings, and the conclusion was unmistakable: I had been working on this propulsion system for weeks. And then, tucked beneath my finalized reports, was another set of logs, identical in every decimal point, every anomaly recorded. I threw the stack onto the floor, the papers fluttering like defeated birds. I paced the length of the room, my movements jerky and agitated, my gaze sweeping over the closed doors as if expecting someone to burst in and correct this grotesque error. "This isn't funny," I hissed to the empty space, the words tasting like ash. I stopped abruptly, pressing the heels of my hands against my eyes, trying to force the impossible sight away, but the resemblance was too perfect, too damning.
